articles.title: US: Student visa issuances fell by -36% in summer 2025; OPT uncertainty among factors affecting international student demand
articles.summary: The US government has renewed its focus on the Optional Practical Training (OPT) programme that allows international students on F-1 visas to gain post-study work experience for one to three years following their studies. Before Secretary of Homeland Security Kristi Noem was replaced in her role on 5 March, she responded favourably to Senator Eric… The post US: Student visa issuances fell by -36% in summer 2025; OPT uncertainty among factors affecting international student demand appeared first
articles.insight:
US universities face sharply declining F-1 visa issuances—down 36% in summer 2025—driven by OPT uncertainty, deterring applicants from India, China, Nigeria, and Brazil who rely on post-study work pathways to justify tuition investment.  
Admissions teams must proactively co-host virtual OPT clarity sessions with US immigration lawyers for Indian and Chinese applicants, explicitly outlining current STEM OPT eligibility, application timelines, and employer sponsorship alternatives under H-1B and cap-gap rules.

articles.title: US visa issuance fell by 36% last summer
articles.summary: New State Department data has revealed a 36% year-on-year drop in US study visa issuance last summer, as stakeholders blame policy volatility under the Trump administration for the "alarming trend". The post US visa issuance fell by 36% last summer appeared first on The PIE News .
articles.insight:
US universities face sharply reduced applicant volume for F-1 visas, especially from India and China, as heightened scrutiny and policy unpredictability under Trump-era rules deter applications and increase visa refusal risks.  
Admissions teams must proactively co-host virtual F-1 visa prep webinars with trusted US immigration lawyers targeting Indian and Chinese high school seniors, covering DS-160 tips, financial documentation, and recent interview trends.

articles.title: Recent Correspondence Between Schmitt and Noem Indicates OPT Reform Still on DHS Agenda
articles.summary: Last week Senator Eric Schmitt (R-Missouri) released recent correspondence he had with DHS Secretary Kristi Noem on the need for practical training reform. In her response to Sen. Schmitt's letter that asked DHS to "conduct a thorough review of the OPT program to begin the process of either reforming or ending OPT," Sec. Noem refers to the Spring 2025 regulatory agenda item regarding practical training posted on September 4, 2025, saying that it will go through the standard APA notice and commen
articles.insight:
Applicant volume from India, China, and Nigeria will likely decline as OPT uncertainty deters F-1 visa applicants fearing post-study work restrictions, especially amid DHS’s Spring 2025 rulemaking targeting STEM and non-STEM practical training pathways.  
Admissions teams must immediately update country-specific webinars for Indian and Chinese agents to clarify current OPT eligibility, highlight alternative pathways like UK Graduate Visa or Australia’s Temporary Graduate (subclass 485), and embed real-time OPT policy alerts into CRM outreach.

articles.title: New Zealand expands post-study work opportunities for international students
articles.summary: In late 2026, New Zealand is rolling out a new Short Term Graduate Work Visa and extending eligibility for the Post Study Work Visa. No set date for the launch of these new visa provisions has been established. New Zealand’s international education strategy favours managed growth over the next few years. The plan is to… The post New Zealand expands post-study work opportunities for international students appeared first on ICEF Monitor - Market intelligence for international student recruitment .
articles.insight:
New Zealand’s planned 2026 Short Term Graduate Work Visa and extended Post Study Work Visa eligibility will intensify competition for high-potential students from India, Nigeria, and Vietnam—especially against UK (Graduate Visa), Australia (Temporary Graduate Visa subclass 485), and US (OPT) pathways—by lowering perceived post-study employment risk.  
Admissions teams should immediately benchmark NZ’s forthcoming visa rules against current UK Graduate Visa duration limits, Australia’s 485 eligibility criteria, and US OPT STEM extensions—and co-develop targeted webinars with immigration advisors to clarify comparative post-study work advantages for each key origin market.

articles.title: Dozens of international students who won Chevening scholarships were denied visas
articles.summary: The UK Home Office rejected a request to grant visas to Chevening scholars from Afghanistan, Cameroon, Myanmar and Sudan, following a previous ban on student visas.
articles.insight:
Applicant volume from Afghanistan, Cameroon, Myanmar, and Sudan will sharply decline for UK universities as Chevening scholars—targeting master’s students on Tier 4 (now Student Route) visas—face systemic refusals amid tightened Home Office scrutiny of high-risk origin regions.  
Admissions teams must immediately flag and proactively support applicants from these four countries with enhanced financial documentation, certified English test scores, and pre-submission visa readiness checks aligned with UKVI’s latest guidance on credibility interviews and maintenance funds.

articles.title: A policy for allowing foreign universities setting up Indian campuses
articles.insight:
Applicant volume from India will likely decline for US F-1, UK Student Route, Australian Subclass 500, and New Zealand Student Visa programs as domestic access to foreign degrees expands under India’s new campus policy.  
Admissions teams should immediately pilot targeted outreach to Indian students in Tier 2–3 cities—offering virtual campus tours, alumni mentorship, and guaranteed scholarship pathways—to differentiate against newly localised degree options.

articles.title: Urgent employability demands push priorities for IBCs in India
articles.summary: International branch campuses in India are moving quickly to align programs with industry needs as employers call for greater role in shaping curriculum. The post Urgent employability demands push priorities for IBCs in India appeared first on The PIE News .
articles.insight:
Applicant volume from India may decline for US F-1, UK Student Route, AU Subclass 500, and NZ Student Visa programs as IBCs offer locally delivered, industry-aligned degrees with lower costs and reduced visa risk.  
Admissions teams should partner with Indian edtech platforms like upGrad or Byju’s to co-develop micro-credentials stackable toward full degrees, explicitly highlighting UK Graduate Visa, US OPT, AU Post-Study Work Rights, and NZ Open Work Visa pathways.

articles.title: Why are more foreign students choosing India for higher education
articles.insight:
Applicant volume for US F-1, UK Student Route, Australian Subclass 500, and New Zealand Student Visa programs may decline as Indian students—historically the largest cohort—increasingly opt for domestic degrees due to lower costs, improved NIRF-ranked institutions, and post-study work reforms like the new 2-year PG diploma pathway.  
Admissions teams should urgently co-develop targeted scholarship bundles with verified Indian university partners (e.g., IITs, DU, JNU) and fast-track conditional offers for high-GPA applicants applying by March, explicitly benchmarking tuition and living cost savings versus India’s top 20 universities.

articles.title: DOS Rulemaking on Subpart C of the Exchange Visitor Regulations
articles.summary: On March 10, 2026, the U.S. Department of State submitted a proposed rule titled Exchange Visitor Rules: Records to the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) for regulatory review. The proposal, which has appeared on the Department’s regulatory agenda since Spring 2021, would replace the current provisions at 22 CFR 62.43 (Extension of program) and 22 CFR 62.45 (Reinstatement to valid program status) to align the regulations with the current functionality of the Student and Exchange Visitor Info
articles.insight:
Applicant volume for U.S. J-1 exchange programs—especially from India, Brazil, and Nigeria—may decline due to heightened documentation burdens and longer processing times under the new Subpart C rules, increasing visa risk for summer 2026 cohorts.  
Admissions teams must immediately train international recruiters on updated J-1 recordkeeping requirements and co-develop pre-submission checklists with designated school officials for Indian and Brazilian applicants applying under the Summer Work Travel and Research Scholar categories.

articles.title: IGNOU extends January 2026 admission deadline for ODL and online programmes till March 30
articles.summary: The Indira Gandhi National Open University has extended the registration deadline for the January 2026 admission cycle for both ODL and online programmes to March 30. Applications are open for undergraduate, postgraduate, diploma, and certificate courses through the official admission portal. Registration for ODL programmes began on December 16, 2025, and for online programmes on December 24, 2025. Applicants must provide a valid APAAR ID and complete the admission process online through the IGN
articles.insight:
IGNOU’s extended deadline increases competition for international students from India, Nigeria, and Bangladesh—especially those holding UK Student Route or Australian Subclass 500 visas—who may now delay applications to compare affordable ODL options against Western degrees.  
Admissions teams in the US, UK, Australia, and NZ should immediately deploy targeted email campaigns to Indian and African applicants who engaged with January intake webinars but haven’t submitted applications, highlighting visa-friendly start dates and scholarship deadlines before March 30.
